bytes.topic: viem тАФ TypeScript-first, lightweight, low-level, replacing ethers in modern stacks
bytes.title: wagmi v2 тАФ Built on viem, Not ethers
bytes.body:
wagmi v2 dropped ethers.js entirely and rebuilt its internals on viem. This isn't just a dependency swap тАФ it changes how you interact with low-level primitives throughout your app.

Every wagmi hook now returns viem-native types. `useBalance` returns a `bigint` balance, not an ethers `BigNumber`. `useReadContract` expects viem's `Abi` type for the `abi` param. Error types are viem's `ContractFunctionRevertedError`, `UserRejectedRequestError`, etc. тАФ structured, typed, and narrowable without string matching.

The `config` object you pass to `WagmiProvider` accepts a `client` built with viem's `createPublicClient` and `createWalletClient` under the hood via `createConfig`. You can extract those clients directly with `getPublicClient(config)` and `getWalletClient(config)` for raw viem calls outside React.

This matters because you can now mix wagmi hooks with direct viem calls sharing the same transport and chain config тАФ no duplicate RPC connections, no type impedance mismatch between layers. Passing ABIs as `const` assertions gives you fully inferred return types end-to-end, from contract ABI to your component's JSX.

bytes.topic: wagmi тАФ React hooks for Ethereum, connector system, TanStack Query integration
bytes.title: wagmi's TanStack Query Integration тАФ Cache, Invalidate, and Prefetch Ethereum Data
bytes.body:
wagmi v2 is built on TanStack Query, which means every hook like `useBalance`, `useReadContract`, and `useBlockNumber` is a query under the hood. You get automatic caching, background refetching, and deduplication for free тАФ no custom state management needed.

Each wagmi hook accepts `query` config to control caching behavior. Set `staleTime: Infinity` for static contract data like token decimals, or `refetchInterval: 4_000` to poll every block. You can also call `queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey })` after a write to immediately refresh dependent reads.

wagmi exports `useQueryClient` and query key factories via `hashFn` and `readContractQueryOptions`, letting you construct precise query keys for targeted invalidation. After a `useWriteContract` mutation confirms, invalidate just the affected balance or allowance query rather than nuking the entire cache.

Connectors (MetaMask, WalletConnect, Coinbase) are first-class in this model too тАФ `useAccount` and `useChainId` sync via wagmi's internal store, while on-chain reads flow through TanStack Query, giving you a clean separation between wallet state and contract data.

bytes.topic: ethers.js тАФ providers, signers, contract interaction, event listening
bytes.title: wagmi v2 тАФ Mapping ethers.js Providers, Signers, and Events to Hooks
bytes.body: wagmi v2 replaces the ethers.js mental model entirely with viem-backed hooks. Forget manually instantiating providers or signers тАФ wagmi surfaces them as `usePublicClient` (read-only, replaces `JsonRpcProvider`) and `useWalletClient` (write-capable, replaces `Signer`). Contract reads use `useReadContract`, which caches results and re-fetches on block updates automatically. Writes go through `useWriteContract`, which returns a `writeContract` function and exposes `isPending`/`isSuccess` state тАФ no manual gas estimation needed. For event subscriptions, `useWatchContractEvent` sets up a filter and fires a callback on each match, cleaning up the listener on unmount so you never leak subscriptions. One sharp edge: `useWalletClient` returns `data` that is async-resolved, so always guard with `if (!walletClient) return` before passing it to a viem write call. The underlying `PublicClient` and `WalletClient` are fully accessible if you need to drop down to raw viem calls for anything wagmi doesn't cover.

bytes.topic: The Graph тАФ subgraphs, indexing blockchain events, GraphQL queries, hosted vs decentralized
bytes.title: wagmi + The Graph тАФ Query Indexed Blockchain History Instead of Scanning Events
bytes.body:
wagmi's `useWatchContractEvent` catches live events, but it can't reach historical data. That's The Graph's job. A subgraph indexes your contract's emitted events into a PostgreSQL-backed GraphQL API тАФ you define the schema in a manifest, deploy it, and query history instantly.

wagmi v2 is built on TanStack Query, so pairing it with `graphql-request` is idiomatic: use `useQuery` directly with The Graph endpoint as the fetcher. Pull `address` from `useAccount()` and pass it as a variable тАФ no custom hooks required.

Hosted Service (`api.thegraph.com`) is free and centralized тАФ fine for dev/staging but deprecated for new subgraphs. The decentralized network (`gateway.thegraph.com`) requires GRT and an API key, but gives you SLA-backed indexing with redundancy. For production wagmi apps, migrate to the decentralized endpoint and gate your key server-side or via an env-prefixed Next.js route handler to avoid leaking it client-side.

Subgraphs eliminate the need for `getLogs` pagination or a custom indexer for most read-heavy use cases.

bytes.topic: Reading On-chain Data тАФ eth_call, getLogs, event filtering, batch requests
bytes.title: wagmi тАФ Reading On-chain Data with useReadContract, getLogs, and Multicall
bytes.body:
wagmi's `useReadContract` maps directly to `eth_call` under the hood тАФ it reads contract state without sending a transaction. For a single slot, pass `address`, `abi`, `functionName`, and `args`. Results are typed via viem's ABI inference, so you get autocomplete on return values.

For event history, use `useWatchContractEvent` for live streaming or viem's `getLogs` via `usePublicClient` for historical queries. Pass `fromBlock`, `toBlock`, and an `event` ABI entry to filter precisely тАФ no topic encoding needed.

Batch multiple reads with `useReadContracts`, which batches calls into a single JSON-RPC `eth_call` using multicall3 by default. Each contract in the `contracts` array returns `{ data, error, status }` independently, so partial failures don't break the batch.

Avoid `useContractRead` (deprecated). Prefer `useReadContract` for single reads and `useReadContracts` for batches. Set `watch: true` on any hook to revalidate on new blocks without manual polling.

bytes.topic: Wallet Connection тАФ WalletConnect, MetaMask, Coinbase Wallet, wallet abstraction
bytes.title: wagmi тАФ Multi-Wallet Support with WalletConnect, MetaMask, and Coinbase Wallet
bytes.body:
wagmi's `createConfig` accepts a `connectors` array where you wire up every wallet you want to support. Each connector is a first-class object from `wagmi/connectors` тАФ no third-party adapter layer needed. `metaMask()`, `coinbaseWallet()`, and `walletConnect()` all implement the same `Connector` interface, so `useConnect` handles them identically: call `connect({ connector })` and wagmi manages the provider switch, chain negotiation, and account exposure automatically.

`walletConnect()` requires a `projectId` from cloud.walletconnect.com and optionally accepts `showQrModal: false` if you're rendering your own modal via `useConnections`. `coinbaseWallet()` supports a `preference` field тАФ set `'smartWalletOnly'` to force EIP-4337 smart accounts, or `'all'` to let users choose.

For wallet abstraction, reach for `useConnectors()` to get the live connector list at runtime and render a dynamic wallet picker. Combined with `useAccount()` checking `isConnected`, you get a fully reactive connection state machine with zero manual event wiring.

bytes.topic: IPFS тАФ content addressing, DHT, pinning, IPFS vs Filecoin, CIDs
bytes.title: wagmi + IPFS тАФ Resolving On-Chain CIDs Without Breaking NFT Metadata
bytes.body: IPFS uses content addressing: a CID is a cryptographic hash of the content itself, so `ipfs://bafkreigh2akiscaild...` always resolves to identical bytes regardless of which peer serves them. When wagmi's `useReadContract` reads `tokenURI` from an ERC-721, it typically returns an IPFS URI тАФ either CIDv0 (`Qm...`, base58 SHA2-256) or CIDv1 (`baf...`, base32 multibase). Browsers don't speak the `ipfs://` scheme natively, so you must rewrite to an HTTP gateway before passing to `fetch` or an `` tag. The DHT (Distributed Hash Table) is how IPFS nodes discover which peers hold a given CID тАФ it's a lookup layer, not a guarantee of availability. Pinning is that guarantee: without an explicit pin, nodes garbage-collect unpinned content. For production, use Pinata or Web3.Storage to pin NFT metadata CIDs. IPFS тЙа Filecoin: IPFS is the content-routing protocol; Filecoin is a separate incentive layer that pays storage providers to hold pinned CIDs under cryptographic proof-of-storage deals. An unpinned CID on IPFS can vanish; a Filecoin deal gives you verifiable persistence. Always validate the CID prefix in wagmi hooks before resolving тАФ a malformed URI silently returns `undefined` from `useReadContract`.

bytes.topic: Writing Transactions тАФ signing, gas estimation, nonce management, transaction monitoring
bytes.title: wagmi Transaction Writing тАФ Simulate, Send, and Monitor in One Flow
bytes.body: wagmi v2 splits transaction writing into three distinct hooks that mirror the actual on-chain lifecycle. Use `useSimulateContract` first тАФ it dry-runs the call via `eth_call` and returns a typed `request` object, catching reverts before spending gas. Feed that `request` directly into `useWriteContract` to get a `writeContract` function and the resulting `hash`. Critically, `useSimulateContract` also handles gas estimation internally; override with `gas` only when you need a ceiling. Nonce management is handled by the connected wallet by default, but you can pass `nonce` explicitly for account abstraction or batching scenarios. Once you have a `hash`, hand it to `useWaitForTransactionReceipt` to poll for confirmation тАФ it exposes `status` (`'pending' | 'success' | 'error'`), `confirmations`, and the full `TransactionReceipt`. Chain the enabled flag on `useWaitForTransactionReceipt` so it only activates post-submission. This three-hook pattern gives you granular error surfaces: simulation errors тЙа send errors тЙа receipt errors.

bytes.topic: ENS тАФ name resolution, avatar, records, reverse resolution
bytes.title: wagmi ENS тАФ Name Resolution, Avatars, and Reverse Lookup in One Hook
bytes.body: wagmi ships first-class ENS support via `useEnsAddress`, `useEnsName`, `useEnsAvatar`, and `useEnsText` тАФ no ethers.js manual calls needed. Forward resolution maps a name like `vitalik.eth` to an address via `useEnsAddress({ name: normalize('vitalik.eth') })`. Always wrap inputs with `normalize` from `viem/ens` to prevent ENS normalization errors at runtime. Reverse resolution flips it: pass a raw address to `useEnsName({ address })` to retrieve the primary name set by the user. For avatars, `useEnsAvatar({ name })` resolves the `avatar` text record and handles IPFS, Arweave, and HTTP URIs transparently. Arbitrary records тАФ Twitter handle, email, URL тАФ are read with `useEnsText({ name, key: 'com.twitter' })`. All four hooks accept a `chainId` prop so you can target ENS on mainnet even when the user's wallet is on a different chain. Combine `useEnsName` + `useEnsAvatar` on the connected account to build a fully resolved identity header with two lines of hook usage.

bytes.topic: Multicall тАФ batching contract reads, reducing RPC calls, gas savings
bytes.title: wagmi Multicall тАФ Batch Contract Reads with useReadContracts
bytes.body:
Every separate `useReadContract` call fires its own `eth_call` RPC request. At scale тАФ think displaying token balances, allowances, and staking positions simultaneously тАФ this hammers your RPC provider and inflates latency. wagmi's `useReadContracts` solves this by batching all reads through the Multicall3 contract in a single `eth_call`.

Pass an array of `contracts` configs тАФ each with `address`, `abi`, and `functionName` тАФ and wagmi routes them through Multicall3 automatically when the chain supports it (virtually all EVM chains do). Results come back as a typed array of `{ result, status }` objects, so you handle individual failures without losing the entire batch.

Set `allowFailure: false` if you need strict all-or-nothing semantics тАФ wagmi will throw if any call reverts. Leave it `true` (the default) for resilient UIs that degrade gracefully per-call. Combine with `multicallAddress` override when targeting chains with a custom Multicall3 deployment. The batching is automatic тАФ no manual ABI encoding needed.

bytes.topic: Testing with Tools тАФ forking mainnet, Hardhat/Foundry test environment, mocking contracts
bytes.title: wagmi тАФ Testing with Forked Mainnet and Mocked Contracts
bytes.body:
wagmi's `WagmiProvider` expects a real or simulated chain. For integration tests, the sharpest approach is pairing wagmi with `viem`'s `createTestClient` over a Hardhat or Foundry forked mainnet тАФ giving your hooks access to live state without touching real funds.

Wrap your test tree with a `WagmiProvider` configured against a `hardhat` or `anvil` transport pointed at your local fork (`http://127.0.0.1:8545`). Use `createConfig` with `chains: [hardhat]` and `transports: { [hardhat.id]: http() }`. Now `useReadContract`, `useWriteContract`, and `useWaitForTransactionReceipt` all resolve against forked state.

For unit tests where you want deterministic responses without a running node, swap the transport for `mock()` from `@wagmi/core/test`. Feed it a `requests` handler array that intercepts specific RPC calls тАФ perfect for simulating reverts, specific balances, or ERC-20 allowance responses. Combine `mock()` transport with `renderHook` from Testing Library to test hook behavior in isolation, asserting on `data`, `error`, and `isPending` transitions without any network dependency.

bytes.topic: Frontend Web3 UX тАФ pending states, failed tx handling, gas estimation errors
bytes.title: wagmi тАФ Handling Pending States, Failed Transactions, and Gas Errors Like a Pro
bytes.body: wagmi's `useWriteContract` and `useWaitForTransactionReceipt` give you fine-grained control over every UX state in a transaction lifecycle. `useWriteContract` exposes `isPending` (wallet prompt open) and `isError` with a typed `error` тАФ check `error.cause` for `UserRejectedRequestError` to distinguish user cancellations from real failures. Once you have a `hash`, pass it to `useWaitForTransactionReceipt` to track `isLoading` (mining) vs `isSuccess` vs `isError` (reverted on-chain). For gas estimation failures, `useEstimateGas` throws a `ContractFunctionExecutionError` before the wallet even opens тАФ catch this to show inline warnings instead of letting the wallet display a cryptic error. Combine `useSimulateContract` before calling `writeContract`: if simulation fails, the button stays disabled and you surface the revert reason directly from `error.cause.reason`. This pattern eliminates the three most common Web3 UX dead-ends: silent wallet rejections, confusing mining spinners, and surprise gas failures at signing time.

bytes.topic: Security тАФ private key never in frontend, signature phishing, approvals management
bytes.title: wagmi Security тАФ Phishing Signatures, Approval Exploits, and Why Your Frontend Holds No Keys
bytes.body:
wagmi never exposes private keys тАФ all signing is delegated to the injected wallet via `useSignMessage`, `useSignTypedData`, or `useSendTransaction`. Your dApp only constructs the payload; the wallet owns execution. The danger is what you put in that payload.

Signature phishing happens when a malicious site uses `useSignTypedData` with a crafted EIP-712 domain that mimics a trusted protocol. Always display the `domain.name` and `domain.verifyingContract` in your UI before calling `signTypedDataAsync` so users can verify the target contract.

Approval exploits are the bigger silent risk. Calling `useWriteContract` with `approve(spender, MaxUint256)` grants unlimited allowance. Instead, pass the exact token amount needed for the transaction. After the operation, prompt users to revoke via a follow-up `approve(spender, 0)` write. Use `useReadContract` to query current allowances before any approval flow тАФ if `allowance >= amount`, skip the approve call entirely to avoid redundant exposure.

For `useSimulateContract`, always check the `request` object before executing тАФ simulation catches revert scenarios but also reveals the exact calldata your user will sign.

bytes.topic: Choosing a Library тАФ ethers.js vs viem vs web3.js, migration considerations
bytes.title: wagmi's Transport Layer тАФ ethers.js vs viem vs web3.js Migration Considerations
bytes.body: wagmi v2 dropped ethers.js entirely and is built on viem as its sole peer dependency. This isn't cosmetic тАФ viem is tree-shakable, ships typed ABI encoding via `parseAbi`/`encodeAbiParameters`, and returns native BigInt instead of ethers' `BigNumber`. If you're migrating a wagmi v1 project, the breakage surfaces immediately in `useContractRead` тЖТ `useReadContract`, `usePrepareContractWrite` тЖТ `useSimulateContract`, and anywhere you passed ethers `Contract` instances or `providers`. Replace ethers utility calls with viem equivalents: `ethers.utils.formatEther` тЖТ `formatEther` from viem, `ethers.utils.parseUnits` тЖТ `parseUnits`. web3.js has no first-class integration path тАФ wagmi never supported it natively, so a migration means rewriting all contract interaction logic to viem's `readContract`/`writeContract` primitives, which the wagmi hooks wrap internally. The upside: viem's strict TypeScript inference means your ABI types flow end-to-end through `useReadContract`'s `data` return without manual casting.

bytes.topic: Contract ABIs тАФ what ABIs are, encoding/decoding, generating typed ABIs
bytes.title: wagmi Contract ABIs тАФ Encoding, Decoding, and Typed Generation
bytes.body:
ABIs (Application Binary Interfaces) define a contract's external surface тАФ function names, argument types, return types, and event signatures. The EVM uses this schema to encode calldata (function selector + ABI-encoded args) and decode return data back into usable values.

In wagmi, every hook that touches a contract тАФ `useReadContract`, `useWriteContract`, `useSimulateContract` тАФ requires an ABI. Wagmi delegates the actual encoding and decoding to viem under the hood. The critical detail: always annotate your ABI `as const`. Without it, TypeScript widens the type to `string`, and wagmi can't infer `functionName` as a union of your contract's methods, narrow `args` to the correct tuple types, or type the return value тАФ everything degrades to `unknown`.

For real projects, skip hand-authored ABIs entirely. Use `@wagmi/cli` with the `foundry` or `etherscan` plugin: `wagmi generate` outputs a `src/generated.ts` with `as const` ABIs and pre-built typed React hooks for every contract function. Your hooks stay in sync with deployments automatically, and you get zero-config type safety across the stack.

For manual encoding outside hooks тАФ multicall payloads, raw transaction construction тАФ reach for viem's `encodeFunctionData` and `decodeFunctionResult` directly.

bytes.topic: RPC Management тАФ rate limits, fallback providers, Alchemy vs Infura vs QuickNode
bytes.title: wagmi RPC Management тАФ Fallback Providers, Rate Limits, and Picking Between Alchemy, Infura, and QuickNode
bytes.body:
wagmi v2 delegates all transport logic to viem тАФ so RPC management lives in your `createConfig` `transports` map, not in React hooks. The critical primitive is viem's `fallback()` transport, which chains multiple providers and automatically retries the next one on any network error or 429 rate limit response.

Alchemy is the default choice for most dApps: generous free tier (300M compute units/month), fast archive access, and reliable `eth_getLogs` with deep block range support. Infura works for simple reads but throttles aggressively on free plans and imposes stricter `eth_getLogs` depth limits. QuickNode shines for latency-sensitive workloads like real-time mempool watching over WebSocket, but costs more at scale.

Pass `rank: true` to `fallback()` to enable latency-based provider ranking тАФ wagmi will automatically prefer the fastest responding RPC across requests. For WebSocket subscriptions (used by `useWatchContractEvent` and `useWatchBlocks`), configure a separate `webSocket()` transport alongside your `http()` fallback; `fallback()` supports mixed transport types. Each `http()` instance accepts `retryCount` and `retryDelay` to tune per-provider retry behavior before escalating down the fallback chain.

bytes.topic: IPFS Pinning тАФ Pinata, web3.storage, NFT.Storage, self-hosted nodes
bytes.title: wagmi + IPFS Pinning тАФ Pinata, web3.storage, and On-Chain CIDs via useWriteContract
bytes.body:
IPFS pinning services ensure your NFT metadata stays retrievable тАФ the CID you write on-chain via wagmi's `useWriteContract` is worthless if the content isn't pinned. The workflow is always: pin first, then write.

**Pinata** is the most battle-tested option. Use their REST API or `@pinata/sdk` to pin JSON metadata server-side (never expose your JWT client-side), then pass the returned `IpfsHash` as an `ipfs://` URI to your contract call.

**web3.storage** (w3up) uses UCAN-based auth and stores to both IPFS and Filecoin. It's more decentralized but requires the `@web3-storage/w3up-client` setup flow тАФ heavier onboarding than Pinata.

**NFT.Storage** is largely legacy тАФ its free tier depends on Protocol Labs funding and should not be used for new production builds.

**Self-hosted Kubo nodes** give full control via the HTTP API at `localhost:5001/api/v0/add`, but you bear the ops burden of availability and garbage collection.

In your wagmi component, the CID flows in as a prop or state after the async pin step, keeping your `writeContract` call clean and side-effect-free.

bytes.topic: Event Indexing тАФ why you can't rely on full node getLogs for production, subgraph vs custom indexer
bytes.title: wagmi Event Indexing тАФ Why getLogs Breaks in Production and What to Use Instead
bytes.body:
wagmi's useWatchContractEvent is perfect for real-time event streaming, but it's a trap for historical data. Internally, it delegates to viem's watchContractEvent, which polls eth_getLogs тАФ and every major RPC provider (Alchemy, Infura, QuickNode) caps that at 2,000тАУ10,000 blocks per request. Try scanning a Uniswap V3 pool from deployment block and you'll exhaust rate limits or timeout before retrieving anything useful.

For production, pick one of two paths:

The Graph (subgraph): Write AssemblyScript mappings, deploy a subgraph, query via GraphQL. wagmi v2 ships TanStack Query as its data layer, so useQuery slots directly in тАФ use it for historical state, useWatchContractEvent for live deltas only.

Custom indexer (Ponder, Envio): TypeScript-native, faster local iteration, full control over storage. Ponder exposes a REST/GraphQL API you wire straight into your wagmi frontend.

The production pattern: index all historical events externally тЖТ hydrate UI via useQuery тЖТ stream new events with useWatchContractEvent and trigger a refetch. Never paginate getLogs client-side тАФ it doesn't scale past a few thousand blocks.
